domains.title: E-Mobility & Automotive
domains.description: High-voltage power systems, battery management, and automotive safety validation

projects.title: Firmware Flashing Automation Tool
projects.tagline: Production GUI tool enabling global EV firmware updates
projects.description: Designed and developed a Qt/C++ desktop GUI application to automate firmware flashing for a multi-controller embedded system used in an enterprise EV power distribution product. The tool simplified complex firmware update workflows and enabled safe, repeatable updates by non-firmware users.
projects.cardOutcome: Replaced multiple CLI-based workflows with one GUI — field technicians now flash firmware without engineering support.
projects.situation: Firmware updates previously required command-line expertise and direct firmware engineering support, making updates time-consuming and error-prone for manufacturing, validation, and field teams. Updating multiple embedded controllers lacked a unified, user-friendly interface.
projects.task: Create a production-grade flashing tool that abstracts low-level firmware update complexity, prevents incorrect sequencing or configuration errors, enables non-engineers to perform firmware updates independently, and improves reliability, efficiency, and scalability.
projects.action: Designed and implemented a Qt/C++ GUI application from the ground up. Integrated CAN-based diagnostic workflows using industry-standard interfaces. Built configuration-driven flashing logic with validation and safety checks. Added real-time logging and error-proofing to reduce manual mistakes. Incorporated cross-site feedback to standardize workflows and usability.
projects.result: 30–50% reduction in firmware flashing time. Enabled independent firmware updates by non-engineers. Eliminated common manual flashing errors. Reduced dependency on firmware engineers for routine updates.

cardMetrics.label: EFFICIENCY
cardMetrics.value: Reduced flashing steps by 30-50%

cardMetrics.label: SCALE
cardMetrics.value: Deployed to U.S. and Poland teams

cardMetrics.label: RELIABILITY
cardMetrics.value: Eliminated manual flashing errors

projects.impact: Standardized firmware update workflows across teams. Improved manufacturing and service readiness. Freed firmware engineers to focus on higher-value development work.
projects.complianceNote: All proprietary software, firmware, system architecture, and internal tooling remain the intellectual property of Eaton. This project is presented externally only at a high-level, non-confidential abstraction.

links.label: Source Code (Internal)
links.note: Available to Eaton employees with appropriate access

projects.title: Hardware-in-Loop (HiL) Validation – Enterprise EV Power Systems
projects.tagline: Hardware-in-Loop validation for a new OEM customer pursuit
projects.description: Performed Hardware-in-Loop (HiL) firmware validation for an enterprise EV power distribution system in support of a new OEM customer program. The work focused on validating safety diagnostics, fault handling, and firmware behavior using automated and manual HiL test methodologies.
projects.cardOutcome: Resolved setup blockers stalling a new OEM program — 40+ fault types verified and regression suite running.
projects.situation: A new OEM customer program required firmware updates beyond an existing baseline. These changes needed structured HiL verification before release. However, global engineering teams were blocked by test setup issues, causing regression delays and preventing automated test execution.
projects.task: Support HiL verification for the new OEM program by executing regression and requirements-based test suites, validating firmware changes related to safety diagnostics and fault handling, and removing test setup blockers impacting distributed engineering teams.
projects.action: Executed Python/pytest-based automated HiL test suites. Performed manual firmware flashing as part of validation workflows. Configured HiL communication interfaces and resolved setup issues blocking automation. Injected hardware-level faults to validate diagnostic detection and logging. Analyzed firmware behavior across 40+ monitored fault conditions. Unblocked remote teams by resolving preconditions preventing long-running test execution.
projects.result: Regression test suites ran to completion without setup failures. 40+ fault types successfully validated. Confirmed reliable fault logging and diagnostic coverage. Eliminated setup issues that were preventing automated test runs.

cardMetrics.label: QUALITY
cardMetrics.value: Validated 40+ fault types

cardMetrics.label: SAFETY
cardMetrics.value: Verified critical safety diagnostics for EV program

cardMetrics.label: UNBLOCKED
cardMetrics.value: Removed setup blockers for global engineering teams

projects.impact: Enabled firmware releases to proceed with confidence for a new OEM program. Reduced downtime caused by failed or blocked automated test runs. Validated safety-critical diagnostics essential for EV deployment. Improved HiL readiness for globally distributed engineering teams.
projects.complianceNote: All firmware, test infrastructure, hardware configurations, and validation artifacts remain the intellectual property of Eaton. This project is presented externally only at a high-level, non-confidential abstraction.

links.label: Source Code / Test Assets (Internal)
links.note: Available to Eaton employees with appropriate access

projects.title: Battery Management System Validation (isoSPI)
projects.tagline: isoSPI validation for high-voltage battery monitoring
projects.description: Validated isoSPI-based communication and diagnostics for a high-voltage battery monitoring system used in enterprise eMobility applications. The work focused on enabling reproducible validation workflows for safety-critical battery monitoring ICs and unblocking teams stalled by ambiguous test procedures.
projects.cardOutcome: Turned a blocking isoSPI ambiguity into a repeatable workflow — engineers now validate independently without SME support.
projects.situation: No reproducible or well-documented workflow existed for validating isoSPI communication in a safety-critical battery management context. Engineers were blocked by unclear sequencing, GPIO behavior, and non-obvious memory interactions, slowing development and creating dependency on subject-matter experts.
projects.task: Develop, validate, and document a reliable isoSPI validation flow that enables independent execution by other engineers, confirms safety-critical communication paths, and removes ambiguity around configuration and memory behavior.
projects.action: Validated isoSPI communication flows between a host controller and battery monitoring IC evaluation hardware. Verified non-volatile memory access and configuration behavior. Evaluated GPIO configuration and protection-related behavior under controlled conditions. Resolved sequencing and configuration ambiguities blocking validation. Created clear, reproducible engineering documentation covering setup, workflow, and validation steps. Served as a technical reference point for cross-team questions on isoSPI and memory behavior.
projects.result: Engineers could execute isoSPI validation independently without SME support. Memory access and configuration behavior validated end-to-end. Ambiguities caused by tooling or version differences were resolved. Validation workflows became repeatable and predictable.

cardMetrics.label: UNBLOCKED
cardMetrics.value: Resolved isoSPI blockers across eMobility team

cardMetrics.label: SAFETY
cardMetrics.value: Validated safety-critical HV battery communication paths

cardMetrics.label: KNOWLEDGE
cardMetrics.value: Created reproducible procedures — eliminated test ambiguity

projects.impact: Unblocked battery system development that had stalled on isoSPI validation issues. Reduced dependency on individual expertise for routine validation. Increased confidence in safety-critical HV battery communication paths. Improved knowledge sharing and validation consistency across teams.
projects.complianceNote: All firmware, validation procedures, documentation, and system details remain the intellectual property of Eaton. This project is presented externally only at a high-level, non-confidential abstraction.

links.label: Source Code / Documentation (Internal)
links.note: Available to Eaton employees with appropriate access

domains.title: IoT & Smart Home
domains.description: Connected devices, wireless protocols, and smart home ecosystem integration

projects.title: Matter & Thread Protocol Evaluation
projects.tagline: Evaluating the next innovation beyond Wi-Fi for Eaton's smart home roadmap
projects.description: Conducted a hands-on evaluation of Matter and Thread protocols to assess readiness beyond Wi-Fi for enterprise smart-home applications. Built a functional multi-ecosystem test environment and translated technical findings into product and platform insights for cross-functional stakeholders.
projects.cardOutcome: Replaced speculation with a working Matter ecosystem — concrete adoption guidance delivered to 5 teams.
projects.situation: The existing smart-home product portfolio was centered on Wi-Fi-based architectures, while competitors had begun shipping Matter-compatible devices. Without internal, hands-on experience with Matter and Thread, teams lacked the technical grounding needed to evaluate adoption feasibility, platform implications, and interoperability risks.
projects.task: Evaluate Matter and Thread as a next-generation connectivity option by building a working Matter + Thread ecosystem, comparing behavior across ecosystems and device types, and identifying platform-level considerations required for adoption.
projects.action: Built a functional Matter + Thread test ecosystem using industry reference hardware and tooling. Commissioned devices and validated behavior across multiple smart-home ecosystems. Compared Wi-Fi-based and Thread-based device characteristics. Evaluated interoperability, commissioning flows, and security considerations at a system level. Benchmarked observations against competitor offerings to identify relative strengths and gaps. Synthesized findings into clear technical and strategic insights and shared them with multiple teams.
projects.result: Established internal, hands-on understanding of Matter and Thread capabilities. Identified key platform considerations affecting adoption readiness. Provided teams with concrete insights grounded in experimentation rather than speculation. Created a shared technical baseline across stakeholders.

cardMetrics.label: STRATEGY
cardMetrics.value: Directly informed wiring device product roadmap

cardMetrics.label: BREADTH
cardMetrics.value: Findings shared with 5 cross-functional teams

cardMetrics.label: COMPETITIVE
cardMetrics.value: Benchmarked against Eve, Leviton, ELEGRP

projects.impact: Informed product roadmap discussions related to next-generation smart-home connectivity. Enabled alignment across product, firmware, platform, and cybersecurity teams. Accelerated decision-making by replacing assumptions with validated technical data.
projects.complianceNote: All internal analyses, platform evaluations, and roadmap-related insights remain the intellectual property of Eaton. This project is presented externally only at a high-level, non-confidential abstraction, consistent with Eaton's confidentiality and ethics guidance.

links.label: Presentations / Findings (Internal)
links.note: Available to Eaton employees with appropriate access

projects.title: Smart WiFi Devices Testing
projects.tagline: End-to-end qualification of 50+ smart home devices before product launch
projects.description: Led end-to-end qualification testing for an enterprise smart-home Wi-Fi device portfolio, ensuring firmware readiness and system interoperability prior to product launch. The work covered provisioning, OTA updates, mobile app integration, and voice-assistant validation across a broad device lineup.
projects.cardOutcome: Caught firmware defects across ~50 devices before launch — no prior test plan existed.
projects.situation: A new firmware release introduced changes to device provisioning, OTA behavior, and application interoperability. No existing test plan covered the updated behavior across the full smart-device portfolio, creating risk ahead of product qualification and launch.
projects.task: Validate the new firmware release across the complete smart-Wi-Fi product line by creating a comprehensive test strategy, executing qualification testing across multiple device categories, and ensuring app and voice-assistant interoperability prior to release.
projects.action: Created and executed a comprehensive test plan covering BLE-assisted provisioning and Wi-Fi onboarding, OTA firmware update flows, device behavior and cloud communication, mobile application interoperability, and voice-assistant integration across major ecosystems. Performed testing across multiple smart-device form factors. Used vendor tooling and RTOS-level debugging to validate firmware behavior. Logged defects and worked directly with firmware engineers to prioritize and resolve issues before release.
projects.result: ~50 devices qualified across multiple product categories. Firmware defects identified and resolved prior to product launch. Full validation coverage achieved across provisioning, OTA, mobile app, and voice control flows.

cardMetrics.label: SCALE
cardMetrics.value: Qualified ~50 devices across product line

cardMetrics.label: QUALITY
cardMetrics.value: Caught firmware defects before product release

cardMetrics.label: COVERAGE
cardMetrics.value: Full BLE, OTA, App, and voice control validation

projects.impact: Prevented firmware defects from reaching customers across the connected-home product line. Enabled a clean product launch with validated device-to-app and voice-assistant interoperability. Increased confidence in firmware quality and system readiness at scale.
projects.complianceNote: All test plans, firmware artifacts, and internal tooling remain the intellectual property of Eaton. This project is presented externally only at a high-level, non-confidential abstraction.

links.note: Available to Eaton employees with appropriate access

projects.title: Remote Diagnostic System for Field Fault Analysis
projects.tagline: Remote diagnostics for a device class with zero prior fault visibility
projects.description: Designed and prototyped a remote embedded diagnostic system to capture real-time signal behavior for a field-deployed device class that previously had no remote visibility. The solution enabled engineers to triage faults remotely, significantly reducing the need for recurring on-site visits.
projects.cardOutcome: First time this device class had remote fault visibility — field engineers can now triage without a site visit.
projects.situation: A specific device class was responsible for a notable portion of miscellaneous field issues. Each incident required a costly on-site visit, as existing tools only captured fault states after failure — not the signal behavior leading up to the fault — making remote root-cause analysis impossible.
projects.task: Build a diagnostic capability that captures real-time, pre-fault signal behavior, enables remote fault triage by engineering teams, and reduces dependency on recurring on-site visits.
projects.action: Developed an embedded diagnostic prototype combining a microcontroller-based data acquisition path with an edge computing node. Implemented real-time signal capture and streaming into structured data logs. Built a lightweight monitoring dashboard for live observation and post-fault analysis. Automated data export to CSV/JSON formats to support engineering workflows. Validated live signal behavior using hardware-assisted debug and monitoring tools.
projects.result: Enabled real-time visibility into pre-fault signal behavior for the first time on this device class. Made remote fault triage possible without requiring physical site access. Eliminated the need for recurring on-site diagnostic visits.

cardMetrics.label: COST
cardMetrics.value: Reduced recurring $3K/visit on-site costs

cardMetrics.label: VISIBILITY
cardMetrics.value: Enabled real-time remote fault capture — first time for this device

cardMetrics.label: COVERAGE
cardMetrics.value: Targeted a device class behind ~15% of field service calls

projects.impact: Reduced recurring ~$3K per on-site visit costs across field incidents. Addressed the core diagnostic gap behind a significant portion of field issues. Established the first remote diagnostic capability for this class of devices.
projects.complianceNote: All firmware, diagnostic tooling, hardware configurations, and analysis artifacts remain the intellectual property of Eaton. This project is presented externally only at a high-level, non-confidential abstraction.

links.note: Available to Eaton employees with appropriate access

domains.title: Firmware Infrastructure & Tooling
domains.description: Build systems, simulation platforms, and modern development workflows

projects.title: Firmware Simulation & Test Infrastructure
projects.tagline: What if firmware teams never needed a prototype board?
projects.description: Designed and enabled a simulation-based firmware validation infrastructure to eliminate dependency on physical hardware for embedded development. The platform accelerated test cycles, reduced hardware costs, and enabled CI-driven validation across multiple embedded teams.
projects.cardOutcome: 4 teams now validate firmware without hardware access — lab debugging time reduced from hours to near-zero per cycle.
projects.situation: Embedded teams relied heavily on physical prototype boards for firmware validation, spending significant time in lab-based debugging. No software-in-the-loop testing existed, making every firmware change dependent on hardware availability and slowing development cycles.
projects.task: Enable firmware validation without physical hardware by simulating embedded platforms and RTOS behavior, supporting automated, repeatable test execution, and integrating simulation into CI workflows.
projects.action: Developed simulation platform configurations to emulate embedded firmware behavior. Executed firmware workloads in a simulated environment to validate functionality without lab access. Identified and resolved simulation gaps blocking accurate firmware behavior. Worked through appropriate channels to improve simulation model coverage. Integrated automated test hooks to enable CI-driven firmware validation. Drove adoption by enabling other teams to independently use the simulation infrastructure.
projects.result: Eliminated dependency on physical prototype boards for routine firmware validation. Reduced lab-based debugging time from hours per cycle to near-zero. Enabled parallel, repeatable test execution through simulation.

cardMetrics.label: COST
cardMetrics.value: $5-7K annual savings

cardMetrics.label: SPEED
cardMetrics.value: 2x-5x faster test cycles

cardMetrics.label: SCALE
cardMetrics.value: Adopted by 4 embedded teams

projects.impact: Estimated $5-7K annual savings across hardware and engineering time. 2x-5x faster test cycles through simulation and automation. Simulation workflows adopted independently by four embedded teams. Improved firmware quality by shifting validation earlier in the development cycle.
projects.complianceNote: All firmware, simulation configurations, infrastructure tooling, and validation artifacts remain the intellectual property of Eaton. This project is presented externally only at a high-level, non-confidential abstraction.

links.label: Documentation / Presentations (Internal)
links.note: Available to Eaton employees with appropriate access

projects.title: Memory-Safe Embedded Firmware Enablement (Rust)
projects.tagline: Can Rust work for safety-critical embedded firmware at Eaton?
projects.description: Led an internal Rust enablement initiative to evaluate memory-safe embedded firmware development on STM32-class hardware. Delivered a working proof-of-concept, demonstrated interoperability with existing C/C++ code, and created onboarding assets to reduce adoption friction for embedded teams.
projects.cardOutcome: First Rust firmware running on Eaton STM32 hardware — memory-safe embedded development proven viable for future projects.
projects.situation: Embedded teams were primarily using C/C++ and wanted to evaluate memory-safe alternatives. There were no internal examples of Rust running on target hardware, no established patterns for mixed-language integration, and no standardized onboarding path for engineers new to Rust.
projects.task: Research and validate Rust feasibility for embedded workflows by prototyping Rust firmware on embedded hardware, exploring integration approaches with existing C/C++ codebases, and creating reusable onboarding and CI foundations to enable team adoption.
projects.action: Built embedded Rust prototypes and validated them on real hardware (proof-of-life firmware). Investigated and demonstrated C/C++ interoperability patterns to support incremental adoption rather than rewrite-everything. Created a starter repository with structured examples and setup guidance for embedded engineers. Implemented CI workflows and added unit-testing foundations to standardize repeatable validation. Delivered internal training content, including Introduction to Rust and related enablement materials.
projects.result: Established the first working Rust firmware proof-of-concept on embedded hardware within the team context. Demonstrated mixed-language feasibility through C/C++ + Rust integration. Enabled faster onboarding through structured documentation, starter repo, and examples. Provided reusable CI and testing patterns so others didn't need to start from scratch.

cardMetrics.label: ONBOARDING
cardMetrics.value: Structured docs and starter repo reduced ramp-up friction

cardMetrics.label: INNOVATION
cardMetrics.value: First working Rust firmware on Eaton STM32 hardware

cardMetrics.label: SAFETY
cardMetrics.value: Established memory-safe path for future embedded projects

projects.impact: Created an adoption foundation for future memory-safe embedded work by packaging examples, onboarding content, and CI templates into reusable assets. Reduced reliance on a single SME by making enablement materials self-serve for engineers. Positioned memory-safe firmware development as a viable path for selected future embedded components and experiments.
projects.complianceNote: All code, enablement assets, internal documentation, and hardware configurations remain the intellectual property of Eaton. This project is presented externally only at a high-level, non-confidential abstraction.

domains.title: Academic & Research
domains.description: Coursework projects and published research from Georgia Tech and SRM University

projects.title: ENS160 Air Quality Sensor Driver for mbed
projects.tagline: Open-source I2C driver contributed back to the mbed community
projects.description: Developed a C++ I2C driver for the SparkFun ENS160 air quality sensor on the mbed LPC1768 as part of Georgia Tech's ECE 4180 Embedded Systems Design course. The driver and a demo application with uLCD display and pushbutton input were published to the mbed open-source repository. Also prototyped a Pi Pico port using the C++ SDK.
projects.cardOutcome: Published a working I2C driver library to the mbed community — open-source contribution from coursework.
projects.situation: The SparkFun ENS160 air quality sensor was recently launched and had no existing C++ driver for the mbed platform. The team wanted to contribute back to the mbed community that had supported their lab experiments throughout the course.
projects.task: Write a C++ I2C driver for the ENS160 sensor on the mbed LPC1768, build a demo application with display and user input, publish the library to the mbed repository, and attempt a Raspberry Pi Pico port.
projects.action: Wrote a C++ I2C driver library for the ENS160 sensor handling register-level communication, air quality index reads, and sensor configuration. Built a demo application integrating the ENS160 with a uLCD-144G2 display and pushbutton input on the mbed LPC1768. Published both the driver library and project code to the mbed open-source repository. Prototyped a Pi Pico C++ SDK port but encountered TinyUSB printf issues in SDK 1.4.0 that blocked full verification.
projects.result: Working ENS160 driver library published and available on the mbed repository. Demo application validated with live sensor reads displayed on uLCD. Pi Pico driver developed but not fully verified due to SDK limitations at the time.

cardMetrics.label: OPEN SOURCE
cardMetrics.value: Driver library published to mbed repository

cardMetrics.label: DEPTH
cardMetrics.value: Register-level I2C driver from datasheet to working code

cardMetrics.label: SCOPE
cardMetrics.value: mbed LPC1768 driver + Pi Pico port attempted

projects.impact: Contributed a reusable open-source driver to the mbed community for a newly launched sensor. Demonstrated register-level embedded driver development from datasheet to published library.

costSavings.remoteDiagnostics: ~$3K per on-site visit reduced (visit frequency varies)
